CalifOrnia
Fair POlitical
Practices Commission
August 30, 199o
David Kirpatrick, Esg.
National Economic Development
and Law center
1950 Addison Street
Berkeley, cA 947 04
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe I-9O-445
Dear !1r. Kirpatrick:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice
regarding the application of the confliqt of interest provisions
of the p5r,iticai- Reform Act (the ,Actr) 1/ to the board members of
the South Berkeley connunity Housing Development corporation. You
have requested this information in your capacity as counsel for
the corporation. Your Ietter does not request advice regarding a
specific decision before the agency but rather seeks general
guidance; therefore, we are treating your request as one for
informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c) (copy
enclosedl . 2 /
OUESTTONS
1. 
How does Regulation 18703.1 apply to the South Berkeley
community Housing Development corporation (hereafter
"corporationri ) ?
2. 
How can the operation of the corporation be rnade manageable
in the event that the actions of the board rnembers of the
coiporation are not exempted from the disqual ification
provisions of the Act by Regulation 7a7o3.L?
1 co,r".rrr"rrt Code Sections 81OOO-91015. AtI statutory references
are to the covernnent Code unless otherwise indicated. 
Corunission
regulations appear at 2 Californj.a Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seo. AlL references to regulations are to Title 
2
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor lrith the
irnnunity provided by an opinion or fornal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 cal. Code of Regs. Section
1832e (c) (3) . )
428 J Street' suite 8oo ' P.o. Box 8o? . sacramento cA 958o4-ogo7 . (916) 3 22.5660


Our Fi■e No. I-90-445
Page 2
CONCLUSTONS
Because a determination on whether Regulation 18703.1 applies
to the board mernbers of the corporation is based on the specific
facts of a decision, we cannot provide you with a definitive
conclusion as to whether Regulation 18703.1 applies to the board
members of the corporation. Hosrever, the board members are not
autonatically disqualified from voting on uatters relating to the
corporation.
FACTS
In December, 1988, the city Attorney of the city of Berkeley
was advised in the Albuouerque Advice Letter, No. A-88-422, that
the nonprofit Housing Development Corporation located in the city
of Berkeley is an !'agencyit subject to the provisJ-ons of the
political 
Reform Act. consequently, the activities of public
officials affitiated with it are subject to the financial
disclosure and disqual ification provj-sions of the Act.
The South Berkeley connunity Housing Development corporation
(SBCHDC) is the same agency referred to in the Atbuquerque Advice
Ietter. 
The corporation serves in an advisory capacity to the
City of Berketey in planning housing and redevelopment projects
for specific sections of the city. 
The relevant facts are as
follows:
1. 
The corporation operates within a portion of the south
Berkeley Area Planninq District consisting of a total area
which is less than ten square miles and a population of less
than 25,000.
2. 
The board members of the corporation are not required to
reside within the planning district or any particular
geographic area.
3. 
None of the seven board members of the corporation are
eLected public officials.
4. 
Six board members have a principal residence within the
jurisdiction of the corporation, but none have their
residence within 300 feet of the boundaries of the property
which is the subject of the corporation,s proposed
developnent.


Our Fi■e No. I-90-445
Page 3
5. 
The principal residences of the six board menbers referred to
above are located on parcels of land not [ore than one-
quarter acre in size.
6. 
A seventh board nember owns commercial property and operates
a sma1l business directly opposite the property to be
developed by the corporation.
ANALYSTS
In order to assist you in providing general guidance to the
board, the general application of the conflict of interest lalrs to
public officials 
is summarized herein, and several advice letters
that nay be helpful to you are enclosed. Specific application of
Regrulation 18703.1 as it nay appty to a specific decision before
the board nembers of the corporation is also summarized.
The purpose for the conflict of j,nterest provisions of the
Act is to ensure that public officials, 
whether elected or
appointed, perform their duties in an inpartial manner, free from
bias caused by their own financial- interests or the financial
j.nterests of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).)
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official 
fron
making, participating in making, or otherwise using hj.s or her
official 
positj.on to influence a governmental decision in which
the official 
has a financial interest. 
An official 
has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonablv foreseeable
that the decision will have a rnaterial financial effect,
distinquishable from its effect on the public qenerallv, 94 the
official, 
or a nember of the officiaL's inmediate fanily, r/ or on
one of the official's 
economic interests, including:
(a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment
worth one thousand dollars (S1,000) or more.
(b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest
worth one thousand dollars ($f,OOO) or more.
3 n, offi"ial,s 
It immediate fanily'r includes his spouse
dependent children. 
(Section 82029. )
and


Our Fi■e No. I-90-445
Page 4
(c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a comnercial lending
institution in the regular course of business
on terms available to the public without
regard to official 
status, aggregating two
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by or promised to the
public official 
within 12 months prior to the
t irne when the decision i,s rnade.
(d) Any business entity in which the public
official 
is a director, officer, partner,
trustee, enployee, or holds any position of
management.
(e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts
aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250)
or lnore in value provided to, received by, or
promised to the public official within 12
rnonths prior to the time when the decision is
made.
sections 871-03 (a) - (e).
Reasonablv Foreseeable
Per our conversation of August 2, L99o, you seek guidance to
help you deternine when dj-squa l if ication of a board nember of the
corpoiation on a specific decision is required. To require
disqualif ication, the financial consequences of a decision nust be
reasonably . foreseeable at the tine a governmental decision is
nade. Foreseeablity depends on the facts of each partj.cular case.
An effect j.s considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a
substantial likelihood that it will occur. certainty is not
required. However, if an effect is only a nere possibility, 
it is
not reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops.
198, copy enclosed.) The Act seeks to prevent roore than actual
conflicts of interest; it seeks to prevent even the appearance of
a possible conflict of interest. 
(witt v. Morrow (L977) 70 CaI.
App. 3d 817, a22.)


Our Fi■e No. I-90-445
Page 5
Irlaterial Financial Effect
In addition, the foreseeable effect on the official's
financial interest nust also be naterial to require
disqualification. 
The Commission has adopted different
regulations offering some specificity to attorneys advising public
officials 
regarding potential conflicts of interest. 
Regulation
18702 (copy encLosed) sets forth the guidelines for determining
whether an official.'s 
economic interest in a decision is
Itmateriallytr affected as required by Section 87103. If the
official's 
financiaf interest is directlv involved in the
decision, then Regulation 18702.1 (copy enclosed) applies to
determine nateriality. 
on the other hand, if the official's
financial interest is indirectlv affected by the decision, then
Regulations fa7o2.2 Lo La7o2.6 (copies enclosed) would apply to
determine whether the effect of the decision is naterial. 
In
particular, Regulation La7o2.5 applies to deterrnine whether the
effect of a decision is rnateriql as to a nonprofit entity such as
a government agency.
PubIic Generallv
Even if it is ascertained that the effect of a particular
decision will have a material- financial effect on the board
members' properties, they nay participate in decisionuraking if the
effect of a decision on their property i,s not distinguishable fron
the effect on the public aeneral.ly. (Section 871o3.) Regulation
18703 clarifies that:
A material financial effect of a governmental
decision on an official's 
interests, as described
in Government code section 87103, is distinguish-
able from its effect on the public Aenera]Iy unless
the decision will affect the official's 
interest in
substantially the same manner as it will affect all
nembers of the public or a significant segment of
the public.
Regulation 18703.


Our Fi■e No. I-90-445
Page 6
For a thorough discussion on the application of
Regulation 18703 to public officials in relation to a
proposed redevelopment plan see the Marsh Advice Letter, No.
I-90-151 (copy enclosed). You may also be interested in the
Kilian Advice Letter, No. A-89-522 (copy encLosed). It
explains that a public officiaL who has a disgualifying
interest as to one component of the decision may be able to
participate in considering the other components provided he
or she has no disqualifying interest $/ith respect to those
other coroponents and the other components will not affect the
results of the decisions for which the pubtic official 
is
disqua 1i f ied.
Srna 11 Jurisdictions
Regulation 18703.1 provides that under certain specified
circumstances the effect of a governrnental decision on a
public official's 
residence is.not distinguishable fron,the
-effect on the public generally in small jurisdictions. a/
Regulation 18703.1 is an exception to the general rules
requiring disqual ification on the basis of an official's
econonic interests. 
It applies only to those decisions
involvinq land use questions which would have an indirect
effect on the principal residence of an elected officer or
public of f ici.aI.
You have asked whether Regulation 18703.1 (copy
enclosed) applies to the governing board members of the
corporation. Because the results of the test will vary
depending on the specific facts of the decision, we cannot
provide you with a definitive conclusion as to whether the
exceptj.on rrould apply to the personal residences of the board
members. The regRrlation does not apply to commerciaf
property. The general application of each subdivision is
discussed belo!,r.
Reg'ulation 18703.1 covers all public officials 
rrhich
include elected and appointed officials 
and designated
empJ.oyees. Therefore, the regulation applies to the
governing board nenbers of the corporation.
A' Regrulation 18703.1 lras adopted by the Comraission in December
1989. The regulation will be subnitted to the Office of
Adrninistrative Law in the near future. However, in the interim,
the Comnission wiLl be advising on the basis of the language of
the new regulation.


Our Fi■e No. ■-90-445
Page 7
Distance from the subject property is a key el.ement in
deternining whether the effect of a decision is naterial.
consequently, a critical 
factor in defining rrsllall
jurisdiction,rr for purposes of the regulation, is geographic
size. Subdivision (a) of the regulation requires that the
public official's 
agency has jurisdiction over a popuJ.ation
of 25, ooo or 1ess, covering a geographic area of ten square
niles or less. 
You stated that the South Berkeley Area
Ptanning District is composed of census tracts 4234, 4235,
4239, and 5240, totalling a combined population of less than
25,ooo and that the total area is less than ten square niles.
Therefore, the requirements of subdivision (a) aPpear to be
satisfied.
A member of a board or commission in a given city or
other sma1l jurisdiction generally has authority to make
decisions which could have a material financial effect on
real property interests. 
Subdivision (b) of the regplation
requiies that the exception be applied only where the.elected
officer or public official 
is required to reside within the
jurisdiction. 
You indicated in your letter that the bylaws
of tne corporation do not provide any requirement that the
board nernbers reside within the planning district 
or any
other particular geographic area. Therefore, the
requirenents of subdivision (b) do not appear to be rnet under
the corporation's existing bylaws .
Subdivision (c) provides that the exenption applies to
elected officers only where they have been eLected in at-
large elections. 
The board members of the board are
appointed, therefore, subdivision (c) does not apply.
As noted earlier, the rnateriality regulations
differentiate between decisions which have a direct effect on
a public officiat's 
reaL property interest (Regulation
La702.1(a) (3), and those with an indirect effect (Regulation
18702.3). By referencing Regulation 18702.1 in subdivision
(d) of the regulation, automatic appJ.ication of the exception
is not available to a public official 
whose principal
resj.dence rri1l be directly affected by a decision before the
aqency. Regulation L8702.1(a) (3) describes the cj.rcunstances
when a public official,'s residence is directly affected by a
decision. Specific facts were not provided to nake a
deterrnination of whether a board member owns property which
is directly affected by the activities of the corporation.
Subdivision (e) requires that the public official,s
property be at least 300 feet frorn the subject property in


Our Fi■e No. I-90-445
Page 8
order for the exception to apply.s/ we have no specific
facts to determine the applicability of subdivision (e).
In subdivision (f) the regulation references the roaximum
residential lot size pennitted in the jurisdiction, 
in order
to elininate the need for a survey of 1ot sizes to implement
the regulation. 
It allows for a residential lot not larger
than riS percent of the median lot size for the jurisdiction.
The principal residences of the board menbers are located on
parcels of land not nore than one-quarter acre in size,
therefore, the requirements of subdivision (f) appear to be
met.
Subdivision (g) provides a def initi,on for principal
residence to ensure that the principat residence exception
for a public official 
or elected officer is limited to a
single Location. we do not have the facts to apply
subdivision (9).
Please note that subdivision (h) clarifies that even if
the above conditions are not net, the effect of a
governnental decision on the principal .residence of an public
otticiaf 
may be determined not to be distinguishable from-the
effect on the public Aenerally in accordance with Regulation
18703 or other regulations .
LeqalIv Reouired Participation
Where a governmental body is unable to act due to the
disqualification of a number of its members, the Act includes
a lirnited exception to allo$, the governmental decision to be
made:
section 87100 does not prevent any public of-
ficial 
from makj.ng or participating in the making
of a governmental decision to the extent his [or
herl participation is legally required for the ac-
tion or decision to be made. The fact that an
official's 
vote is needed to break a tie does not
rnake his [or her] participation 1egaI1y required
for purposes of this section.
Section 87101.
5 fhi= standard was taken from Regulation 18702.3(a), which
provides that an indirect effect on a public official,s real
property interest within 3OO feet of the subject property is
deened to be material unLess the official can show that the
decision has no financial effect on the real property interest.
In other words, such close proxirnity would require
disqualification unless the pubric offi.ciar cin denonstrate that
the effect on his or her property is no different frorn the effect
on nost other property owners, as required by the rrgenerictr public
generally ruIe.


Our Fi■e No. ■-90-445
Page 9
Regrulation 18701 clarifies application of section 87101. The
provisions of Regulation 18701 specify the procedures to be fo1-
lowed whenever a public official 
is required to participate in a
decision despite a disgualifying interest. 
The reg'ulation states
that a public official 
is not leqallv reouired to participate for
the purpose of breaking a tie, establishing a quorum where there
are sufficient non-disqual ified nembers to take action, or where
an alternative source of decision exists. 
(Regulation 18701, copy
enclosed. )
whiLe a reinstated public official 
can participate in public
neetincrs of the agency to vote or nake a notj,on, he or she is
precluded from discussing or atternpting to persuade other public
officials 
regarding the decision in private meetings prior to the
public vote on the decision. only where the public official 
is
required by law to participate in the decision in closed session
will he or she be allowed to do so. (For a thorough discussion
see lCe Advice Letter, No. A-90-355, and the Hill Advice Letter,
No. A-87-110, copies enclosed. )
I trust this letter provides you with the quidance you
reguested. If you have any further questions regarding this mat-
ter, please contact me at (916) 322-59OL.
s incerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting ceneral, Counsel

By: Luisa Menchaca
counsel, LegaI Division

FACTS In December, 1988, the city Attorney of the city of Berkeley was advised in the Albuouerque Advice Letter, No. A-88-422, that the nonprofit Housing Development Corporation located in the city of Berkeley is an !'agencyit subject to the provisJ-ons of the political Reform Act. consequently, the activities of public officials affitiated with it are subject to the financial disclosure and disqual ification provj-sions of the Act. The South Berkeley connunity Housing Development corporation (SBCHDC) is the same agency referred to in the Atbuquerque Advice Ietter. The corporation serves in an advisory capacity to the City of Berketey in planning housing and redevelopment projects for specific sections of the city. The relevant facts are as follows: 1. The corporation operates within a portion of the south Berkeley Area Planninq District consisting of a total area which is less than ten square miles and a population of less than 25,000. 2.
